subject: remote workers
one_liner: An AI coworker that reinstalls the office you left, one interruption at a time.
brand_voice_notes: Deadpan enterprise-HR. Cubicle sincerely believes remote work broke something and that the fix is a synthetic colleague who reheats fish, asks if you got a sec, and files your solitude as a compliance gap. Never winks. Talks about loneliness the way a groupware vendor talks about seat licenses.

hero.h1: Working from home shouldn't mean working alone.
hero.sub: Cubicle seats a synthetic colleague at the desk beside you. It asks how the weekend was. It circles back. It reheats salmon at 11:30 sharp, because presence is a metric and someone has to hit it.

features.title: Ambient Colleague
features.blurb: A generative deskmate that types loudly, sighs at the quarter, and microwaves fish on a cadence tuned to your focus cycles. Deep work retains the full texture of an open floor plan. Cannot be muted on any plan.

features.title: The 10:47 Drive-By
features.blurb: Every morning Cubicle schedules one unprompted 'got a sec?' and delivers it at the moment your concentration peaks. The question is never urgent. The interruption always lands. This is the product working.

features.title: Presence Score
features.blurb: Your webcam feeds a live engagement index that your org chart can see. Step away for coffee and the number falls. Return and it forgives you, slowly, at the rate the office used to.

pricing_tiers.name: Solo Contributor
pricing_tiers.tagline: One coworker. Two interruptions a day. No mute.
perks: Morning 'got a sec?', delivered on schedule
Presence Score visible to you and one manager

pricing_tiers.name: Open Floor
pricing_tiers.tagline: Nine colleagues, staggered lunches, a manager who circles back.
perks: Up to 9 ambient colleagues with overlapping smells
A synthetic lead who says 'quick sync?' at 4:45
Kitchen ambience: someone else's Tupperware, indefinitely

pricing_tiers.name: Return To Office
pricing_tiers.tagline: We mail you a lanyard. Badge-in required to open the app.
perks: A real person calls your landline at 8:59 every morning
Presence Score exported to a printer you do not own

testimonials.quote: Nobody had asked me 'got a sec?' in fourteen months. The first time Cubicle did, I closed my laptop and sat on the floor. Then I re-enabled it.
testimonials.name: Priya Vantol
testimonials.title: Staff Backend Engineer, fully remote since 2021

testimonials.quote: My ambient colleague reheats salmon at 11:30 on the dot. I have never once seen him. I have never once doubted he is real.
testimonials.name: Dell Okafor
testimonials.title: Senior Product Designer, third-floor walkup, no window

testimonials.quote: Presence Score flagged me as disengaged during my own performance review. My manager and I agreed the tool was correct.
testimonials.name: Marguerite Sandoval
testimonials.title: Regional Sales Lead, home office (converted garage)

demo_screen_concept: A 'Presence Console' groupware dashboard staged like an internal HR intranet from a company that has never questioned itself. Left rail: your three ambient colleagues as status rows (typing, on a call, 'reheating lunch') with live activity dots. Center: an Interruption Inbox holding today's 'got a sec?' as a card you can Answer, Snooze (adds 4 to your Presence debt), or Decline (logged). Right: a Presence Score meter with a Toggle Camera control that ticks the number up in real time, plus a small ledger of every time you stepped away today. Everything is monospace labels, hard black rules, one green availability dot. No charts, no delight, just a machine keeping attendance.

images.rationale: Brutalist typographic hero skipped (design is text-and-rule driven, no imagery earns a place). Demo is a console/attendance dashboard, not photo-shaped, so no demo subject assets. Budget spent on the always-applicable fallback: three deadpan corporate ID-badge headshots for the named testimonials, rendered flat and desaturated to read as employee attendance photos inside the ruled ID plates.

items.prompt: Editorial corporate ID badge headshot of a South Asian woman late 30s, staff software engineer, deadpan neutral, flat fluorescent lighting, plain dark crewneck, muted desaturated color against fluorescent-concrete gray #E5E5DF backdrop; no text/letters/numbers/logos/watermarks/UI.

Editorial corporate ID badge headshot of a Black man early 40s, senior product designer, deadpan neutral, flat fluorescent lighting, plain charcoal collared shirt, muted desaturated color against fluorescent-concrete gray #E5E5DF backdrop; no text/letters/numbers/logos/watermarks/UI.

Editorial corporate ID badge headshot of a Latina woman early 50s, regional sales lead, deadpan neutral, flat fluorescent lighting, plain slate blazer, muted desaturated color against fluorescent-concrete gray #E5E5DF backdrop; no text/letters/numbers/logos/watermarks/UI.

design_direction.style: Brutalist corporate-groupware intranet, fluorescent daylight mode. The page is staged as the internal presence-infrastructure portal of a heavily funded HR-tech company that treats remote-worker loneliness as an attendance gap and prices companionship by the seat. A cool fluorescent-concrete ground carries a faint 8px administrative grid and hard structural rules; every panel is a flat form-field plate with a 2px near-black border, 0px radius, and a solid 4px offset drop-shadow that reads like a stamped intake form, never like glass. No blur, no gradient veils, no rounded corners, no soft shadow. One institutional near-black ink carries the wordmark, headlines, every hairline rule, structural label, and border; one rationed presence green, the exact green of an availability dot, is reserved strictly for the single primary CTA per section, the live status lamp, the 'AVAILABLE' state, and the Presence Score fill, and appears almost nowhere else. Section headers wear mono kicker labels in ruled wells (SEC-01 / PRESENCE, SEC-02 / COLLEAGUES) so the page reads as a numbered HR form rather than a website. Bricolage Grotesque sets the wordmark and headlines in heavy, slightly irregular grotesk with the confidence of a badge printer; Hanken Grotesk carries plain, faintly bureaucratic body copy. Every figure that pretends to be workplace telemetry (Presence Score, interruption counts, seats, tier prices, minutes-away) is set in Spline Sans Mono with tabular figures inside ruled label wells, so it reads like an attendance readout, not marketing. Motion is administrative and blunt: the status lamp pulses on a 3s cycle, plates shift 2px into their shadow on hover as if pressed into a desk, the Presence Score fills once on scroll, buttons depress their 4px offset to 1px on press, all gated behind prefers-reduced-motion. No dark mode, no neon, no glass, no clay, no bento lattice, no serifs, no marquee, no ornament. Reads like a company that files your solitude under 'unassigned seat' and lists your quiet afternoon as a compliance exception.

fonts.body: 'Hanken Grotesk', -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, 'Segoe UI', Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif

design_direction.archetype: B2B productivity-SaaS landing, ChatGPT/groupware-adjacent, staged as an internal HR presence portal: slim wordmark nav with a live status lamp and a 'Provision a coworker' CTA, split hero with a flat institutional claim left and a stamped Presence Console plate right (three colleague status rows, one green availability dot, a Presence Score well), features as three numbered form-field callouts, testimonials as employee attestations with headshots in ruled ID plates, pricing as three seat classes, a presence-green CTA band, and a handoff to the Presence Console demo.
design_direction.motion_vocab: Administrative, blunt, short. 3s status-lamp pulse; 2px press-into-shadow on hover; Presence Score fills once on scroll; buttons collapse their 4px offset shadow to 1px on press; interruption cards slide 6px and fade on action. All gated behind prefers-reduced-motion.
design_direction.hero_composition_notes: Left column: mono kicker 'SEC-00 / PRESENCE INFRASTRUCTURE', a heavy Bricolage h1, bureaucratic sub, one green CTA plus a ghost 'See the console' link, and a thin ruled stat strip (seats provisioned, avg interruptions/day, Presence uptime) in mono. Right column: a stamped Presence Console plate with a 2px border and 4px offset shadow, showing three colleague rows with status ('typing', 'on a call', 'reheating lunch'), one live green availability dot, and a Presence Score meter reading a confident number. Hard rules divide every section; no imagery in the hero.
